results.possibleIdentity: Tadeusz K. Krauze (1934–2019), also known as Tad Krauze, was a Polish-American sociologist and mathematician who bridge the gap between technical sciences and social theory. Born in Warsaw, he earned a Master's degree in mathematics from the University of Łódź and subsequently worked at the Institute of Mathematical Machines in Warsaw, where he gained early experience with computers. In 1958, he moved to the United States to specialize in mathematical psychology at the University of Michigan before pursuing graduate studies at Columbia University and New York University (NYU), where he received his Ph.D. in sociology in 1973. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was active in the New York academic and intellectual scene, serving as a Visiting Lecturer at Haverford College (1970–1971) and eventually becoming a Professor of Sociology at Hofstra University, where he taught for over 40 years. His work frequently utilized cybernetics and mathematical modeling to analyze social phenomena, particularly in the fields of social stratification and the sociology of science. He is notably associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) due to his multidisciplinary background in mathematics, computers, and sociology, and he was professionally linked to Rutgers University (specifically the Newark campus), consistent with the location and technical fields specified in E.A.T. membership records.
